3.1.title: Arjuna's challenge: "why action then?"
3.1.body: Arjuna throws Krishna's own words back: "if intellect (बुद्धि) is better than action (कर्मणः ज्यायसी), why are you ordering me into THIS terrible action (घोरे कर्मणि)?" The verse is structurally a conditional (यदि ... तत्) — premise + accusation. The accusative मा ("me") drives the verb नियोजयसि ("you engage") — Arjuna grammatically positions himself as the object of Krishna's coercion.

3.1.title: Two vocatives, two postures
3.1.body: जनार्दन ("agitator of people") and केशव ("slayer of Keśin") frame the verse — both are Krishna's warrior-epithets. Arjuna is naming Krishna as a violent god while objecting to the violent task. The vocatives carry the irony.

3.2.title: इव × 2 = polite hedge
3.2.body: व्यामिश्रेणेव ... मोहयसीव — both clauses use इव ("as if"). Arjuna doesn't say Krishna's words ARE confused or that they ARE deluding him; he says they SEEM to be. This is grammatical politeness in protest — the इव lets Arjuna name the problem without quite accusing his teacher.

3.2.title: येन-clause: instrumental of means to a goal
3.2.body: येन श्रेयो अहम् आप्नुयाम् — "by which I may attain the higher good." येन is तृतीया (instrumental) of the relative pronoun, anticipating Krishna's तेन-answer that never quite comes as a single clean answer. The whole chapter is the तेन.

3.3.title: द्विविधा निष्ठा — the chapter's thesis
3.3.body: Krishna names two stances (निष्ठा): ज्ञान-योग for सांख्यas (the analytical) and कर्म-योग for योगिनs (the active). The two तृतीयाs (ज्ञान-योगेन, कर्म-योगेन) parallel two षष्ठीs (सांख्यानाम्, योगिनाम्) — instrument-and-bearer for each path.

3.3.title: पुरा ... मया — backward reference
3.3.body: पुरा प्रोक्ता मया = "declared by me long ago." Krishna gestures to a teaching prior to this conversation (cosmic, not narrative — "long ago" means at creation, not earlier in Arjuna's life). मया is तृतीया-agent of the passive PPP प्रोक्ता.

3.4.title: Two negations, one false equation
3.4.body: न ... न च ... — Krishna refutes two errors in parallel: (a) non-starting (अनारम्भात्) doesn't yield नैष्कर्म्य (action-lessness); (b) renouncing (संन्यसनात्) alone doesn't yield सिद्धि. Both उनद्योगस् (non-doing as a path) get pañcamī (ablative-of-means) — "by/from non-action."

3.4.title: नैष्कर्म्य ≠ अकर्म
3.4.body: Critical distinction the rest of the chapter unpacks: नैष्कर्म्य is a STATE (transcendence of binding action), अकर्म is mere non-doing. The verse rejects the simple equation. The chapter's whole pedagogy is: act, but reach the state.

3.5.title: अवशः — the helpless verb
3.5.body: कार्यते अवशः कर्म = "forced (अवशः, "without will") into action." The passive कार्यते ("is being made to do") shifts agency away from the person to प्रकृति-जैः गुणैः (instrumental: "by qualities born of prakṛti"). No one stands outside the action-stream.

3.5.title: क्षणम् अपि — "even for an instant"
3.5.body: Krishna's strongest claim: not even a moment of true non-action exists. क्षणम् (accusative of time) + अपि (emphatic) + जातु ("ever") + तिष्ठति अकर्मकृत् ("remains an actor of non-action") — quadruple emphasis to crush the renunciate's escape hatch.

3.6.title: Mithyācāra — the hypocrite frame
3.6.body: मिथ्याचारः सः उच्यते = "he is called a false-doer." The verse skewers a specific kind of fake renunciate: organs (कर्मेन्द्रियाणि) restrained outwardly, but mind (मनसा) wandering after sense-objects (इन्द्रिय-अर्थान्). The hypocrite suppresses behavior while indulging thought.

3.6.title: Two participles describing one person
3.6.body: स्मरन् ("remembering / dwelling on") and विमूढ-आत्मा ("whose self is deluded") both modify the subject यः ("who"). Both are nominative; participial chain describing the false-doer. आस्ते ("sits") is the finite verb — the only one anchoring the verse.

3.7.title: The contrasting model — असक्तः कर्मयोगी
3.7.body: Opposite of 3.6: same setup (इन्द्रियाणि नियम्य — having restrained the senses), but the action is genuinely undertaken (कर्म-योगम् आरभते) and the agent is unattached (असक्तः). असक्तः is nominative agreeing with सः — the unattached-one is the subject.

3.7.title: Two absolutives, two clauses
3.7.body: नियम्य ("having restrained") chains the first clause to the second; आरभते ("undertakes") is the only finite verb. Sanskrit packs the whole "first restrain, then act" sequence into one sentence via the absolutive — no "and then" needed.

3.8.title: Imperative + comparative — the direct order
3.8.body: कुरु (लोट् 2sg of √कृ): "do (it)!" Then the justification: कर्म ज्यायः अकर्मणः = "action is better than inaction." The comparative ज्यायः governs ablative अकर्मणः (पञ्चमी). Krishna closes the loop opened in 3.1.

3.8.title: शरीर-यात्रा — the body-journey argument
3.8.body: Pragmatic clincher: "even the journey of the body (शरीर-यात्रा) would not succeed for you (ते न प्रसिद्ध्येत्) from inaction." Even mere survival is action. विधिलिङ् प्रसिद्ध्येत् ("would succeed") expresses the counterfactual.

3.9.title: यज्ञ-अर्थम् — the sacrifice exception
3.9.body: Krishna's pivot: ALL action binds (कर्म-बन्धनः) EXCEPT action done for sacrifice (यज्ञ-अर्थात् कर्मणः अन्यत्र = "otherwise than action for the sake of sacrifice"). The कृत्यर्थ adverbial -अर्थम् marks the saving exception.

3.9.title: Two imperatives, one program
3.9.body: तत्-अर्थम् कर्म ... समाचर = "perform action FOR THAT (sacrifice-)PURPOSE." समाचर is लोट् 2sg of सम्-आ-√चर. मुक्त-सङ्गः ("with attachment released") is the बहुव्रीहि modifying the implicit subject "you."

3.10.title: Cosmogonic frame — Prajāpati's gift
3.10.body: Sentence reads: "having created (सृष्ट्वा) progeny along with sacrifice (सह-यज्ञाः प्रजाः), Prajāpati of old (पुरा) said: by this (अनेन) shall you multiply (प्रसविष्यध्वम्); let this be your wish-fulfilling cow (इष्ट-काम-धुक्)." The absolutive सृष्ट्वा chains the cosmogonic moment to the cosmogonic command.

3.10.title: इष्ट-काम-धुक् — the metaphor sealed
3.10.body: धुक् ("milker") from √दुह् + क्विप् — agentive consonant-stem. "Milker of desired wishes" = कामधेनु, the cow that gives whatever is asked. Sacrifice is being framed as the cosmic supply-chain.

3.11.title: Reciprocal भू-causative
3.11.body: देवान् भावयत = "nourish the gods" (causative of √भू, लोट् 2pl: "cause-to-be"). Then देवाः भावयन्तु वः = "may the gods nourish you" (लोट् 3pl, same verb). The cosmic loop is encoded in matched verb-forms — Krishna writes it grammatically as a reciprocity.

3.11.title: परस्परं भावयन्तः — the participle that holds the chain
3.11.body: परस्परं ("each other") + भावयन्तः ("nourishing," present participle nom. pl.) — the participle agrees with "you-and-the-gods." The whole verse hangs on this participial bond between humans and devas.

3.12.title: स्तेन एव सः — "he is a thief, period"
3.12.body: Krishna's verdict on the un-sacrificing eater: तैः दत्तान् अप्रदाय एभ्यः यः भुङ्क्ते स्तेनः एव सः = "who eats without giving back to those who gave is a thief, period." एव is emphatic — no qualification, no wiggle. The eater-without-offering is in cosmic debt.

3.12.title: An absolutive of NOT-doing
3.12.body: अप्रदाय = "not-having-given-back." -य absolutive of प्र-√दा with negation prefix अ-. The negative absolutive is rare in Sanskrit but precise here: the verse is about the failure to perform a return.

3.13.title: Two parallel clauses, two fates
3.13.body: (a) यज्ञ-शिष्ट-अशिनः सन्तः मुच्यन्ते सर्व-किल्बिषैः = "eating sacrifice-remnants, the good are freed from all sins" (तृतीया-of-separation: "freed FROM"). (b) पापाः ये पचन्ति आत्म-कारणात् ते अघं भुञ्जते = "the sinful who cook for their own sake — they eat sin." Same verb (eating), opposite cosmic accounting.

3.13.title: आत्म-कारणात् — "for their own sake"
3.13.body: ablative of motivation (पञ्चमी): "from the cause-that-is-the-self." Cooking driven by self-interest, not as offering, is what makes the eating sinful. The verse names the inner orientation as the differentiator, not the outward act of cooking.

3.14.title: The chain — four ablatives in succession
3.14.body: अन्नात् भूतानि ("beings from food") → पर्जन्यात् अन्न-संभवः ("food's origin from rain") → यज्ञात् पर्जन्यः ("rain from sacrifice") → कर्म-समुद्भवः ("sacrifice arises from action"). Each link a ablative-of-source. Sanskrit's case system makes the causal chain readable at a glance.

3.14.title: Pedagogical wrap-up of the यज्ञ argument
3.14.body: This verse + 3.15 are Krishna's tight summary of the sacrifice-cosmos. He's pre-empting any "why bother with action?" objection by showing action is the bottom of the chain that ends in beings' food.

3.15.title: Brahma-Akṣara-Yajña — the three-step ascent
3.15.body: कर्म ब्रह्म-उद्भवम् ("action arises from Brahman") → ब्रह्म अक्षर-समुद्भवम् ("Brahman arises from the imperishable"). Two compounds, both ablative-derivative (-उद्भव). The chain bottoms out in अक्षर (imperishable). Hence universal Brahman is forever established in यज्ञ — the closing claim of 3.15.

3.15.title: तस्मात् — the cosmic therefore
3.15.body: तस्मात् सर्व-गतं ब्रह्म नित्यं यज्ञे प्रतिष्ठितम् — Krishna lands the metaphysical hammer: BECAUSE of that chain (तस्मात्), all-pervading Brahman is permanently grounded in यज्ञ. The locative यज्ञे = "in sacrifice."

3.16.title: Bahuvrīhi triple insult
3.16.body: Krishna names the non-sacrificer with three बहुव्रीहि compounds: अघायुः ("whose life is sin"), इन्द्रिय-आरामः ("who delights in the senses"), मोघं जीवति ("lives in vain"). Three different ways of saying: this person's existence is wasted.

3.16.title: चक्रम् — the cycle metaphor
3.16.body: एवं प्रवर्तितं चक्रं न अनुवर्तयति इति यः = "who does not follow this set-rolling wheel." चक्रम् ("wheel") is here cosmic, not vehicular — the rotation of cosmic sacrifice (the अन्न-पर्जन्य-यज्ञ-कर्म cycle from 3.14). To opt out is to step off the wheel into वैरिनस् against the universe.

3.17.title: Three -तृप्त predicates for the realized one
3.17.body: आत्म-रतिः ("whose delight is in the self"), आत्म-तृप्तः ("satisfied in the self"), आत्मनि सन्तुष्टः ("content in the self") — three predicate adjectives stacked, each progressively finer. The locative आत्मनि ("in the self") on the third entry shifts grammar but not direction.

3.17.title: The exemption clause
3.17.body: तस्य कार्यं न विद्यते = "there is no duty (कार्य) for him." Krishna will SOON say (3.19-20) that even this person should act for लोक-सङ्ग्रह — so this is the apparent exception that 3.19+ overrules. Pedagogy: state the apparent exemption clearly, then revoke it.

3.18.title: Four negations, one conclusion
3.18.body: न तस्य कृतेन अर्थः ... न अकृतेन ... न कश्चित् अर्थ-व्यपाश्रयः — "no purpose (अर्थ) from doing, none from not-doing, none in seeking anything from any being." The realized one is grammatically built out of negations. The verse uses तृतीया कृतेन / अकृतेन (instrumental: "by doing" / "by not-doing") for the means-by-which-he-might-get-purpose.

3.18.title: अर्थ-व्यपाश्रय — the cling
3.18.body: अर्थ-व्यपाश्रयः = "having recourse to / clinging to a purpose." बहुव्रीहि compound: "one whose recourse-to-purpose is..." Krishna says even THIS — even subtle dependence on outcomes through any being — is absent in the realized.

3.19.title: तस्मात् + imperative: the practical landing
3.19.body: तस्मात् असक्तः सततं कार्यं कर्म समाचर = "therefore, unattached, always perform the duty-to-be-done." The triple modifier (असक्तः + सततं + कार्यं) constrains कर्म तightly: unattached, constant, obligatory. समाचर is लोट् 2sg.

3.19.title: The promise — परम् आप्नोति
3.19.body: Second half: असक्तः हि आचरन् कर्म परम् आप्नोति पुरुषः = "for, performing action unattached, a person attains the supreme." आचरन् is present participle (शतृ) nom. sg. — "while-performing." The participle does the work of an English "by doing X, one attains Y" structure.

3.20.title: जनकादयः — the precedent
3.20.body: Krishna names Janaka and others (जनकादयः, बहुव्रीहि: "those of whom Janaka is the first") as having reached संसिद्धि (perfection) BY ACTION (कर्मणा एव). One of the rare verses where Krishna marshals historical precedent. The तृतीया कर्मणा is instrumental-of-means: through action.

3.20.title: लोक-सङ्ग्रह — the key term introduced
3.20.body: लोक-सङ्ग्रहम् ... सम्पश्यन् कर्तुम् अर्हसि = "considering even the holding-together of the world, you ought to act." लोक-सङ्ग्रह is BG 3's signature concept — the rationale for the realized to still act, returns repeatedly in 3.21, 3.25.

3.21.title: श्रेष्ठ defines the norm
3.21.body: यद् यद् आचरति श्रेष्ठः तत् तद् एव इतरः जनः = "whatever the best does, that and that alone do other people" (the यद्-यद् / तद्-तद् doublet — Sanskrit's "whatever X, exactly that Y"). श्रेष्ठ ("best, foremost") is the subject — the well-placed person sets the conduct-template.

3.21.title: स यत्-प्रमाणं ... लोकः तद् अनुवर्तते
3.21.body: "Whatever standard (प्रमाणम्) he establishes, the world follows that." अनुवर्तते is लट् 3sg ātmanepada — "follows along." Krishna's pedagogy of social influence: leadership encodes itself in mass behaviour grammatically as well as actually.

3.22.title: Krishna's first-person admission
3.22.body: न मे ... अस्ति कर्तव्यं त्रिषु लोकेषु किञ्चन — "there is nothing to be done by me in the three worlds." मे is षष्ठी/चतुर्थी ("by/for me"). किञ्चन = "anything whatsoever." Krishna includes HIMSELF in the लोक-सङ्ग्रह argument: even he has no need, yet he acts.

3.22.title: वर्ते एव च कर्मणि
3.22.body: "And yet I AM engaged in action" (locative कर्मणि = "in action"). वर्ते is लट् 1sg ātmanepada of √वृत् — "I turn / am engaged." The एव ("indeed") underscores the apparent paradox the next two verses unpack.

3.23.title: Counter-factual विधिलिङ् — if I were not to act
3.23.body: यदि अहं न वर्तेयं ... कर्मणि = "if I were not to engage in action." वर्तेयम् is विधिलिङ् 1sg — the optative does the conditional. Then: "people would in every way follow my path" (मम वर्त्म अनुवर्तन्ते मनुष्याः). The grammar of the conditional carries the cosmological stake.

3.23.title: अतन्द्रितः — "undistracted, ever-alert"
3.23.body: बहुव्रीहि नञ् (negative-prefix-with-PPP-stem): "one whose laziness has departed" / "undistracted." The negation is built into the morphology — अतन्द्रित is a single adjective doing what English needs two words for.

3.24.title: Three विधिलिङ्s = cosmic counterfactual cascade
3.24.body: उत्सीदेयुः ... कुर्याम् ... स्याम् ... उपहन्याम् — four विधिलिङ्s in one verse, all 1sg or 3pl conditional. Sanskrit's optative as the natural mood for "if-then-then-then" causal cascades. Krishna spells out what NOT acting would destroy: worlds collapse, vārṇa-mixture, beings ruined.

3.24.title: सङ्करस्य कर्ता — the worst epithet
3.24.body: "I would be the doer of confusion/admixture" (सङ्करः = "caste-confusion" specifically, the deepest social-cosmic dread in the Gītā's ethical universe). सङ्करस्य is षष्ठी governing कर्ता. Krishna names the specific social catastrophe non-acting would cause.

3.25.title: Same action, different consciousness
3.25.body: सक्ताः कर्मणि अविद्वांसः ... कुर्वन्ति = "the unwise, attached to action, act"; कुर्यात् विद्वान् तथा असक्तः = "let the wise act in the same way, but unattached." Same verb (√कृ), same external action — the difference is consciousness (सक्त vs असक्त). Krishna is the early proponent of action-as-meditation.

3.25.title: चिकीर्षुः — desiderative of √कृ
3.25.body: "Desiring to do." Sanskrit's desiderative (सन् pratyaya, reduplication + -इष): चि-कीर्ष-. The wise person ACTS for लोक-सङ्ग्रह AS IF a wanter-of-the-deed — engaged, motivated, but without personal stake.

3.26.title: Don't break their schema
3.26.body: न बुद्धि-भेदं जनयेत् अज्ञानाम् कर्म-सङ्गिनाम् = "let one not produce intellect-confusion in the unwise who are attached to action." बुद्धि-भेद (षष्ठी तत्पुरुष: "splitting of intellect") is the danger — preaching detachment to the unprepared just unsettles their motivation.

3.26.title: जोषयेत् — "let him make them enjoy"
3.26.body: लट् causative: "cause to take pleasure in." The wise one's pedagogical move: don't disrupt; instead, perform actions yourself and inspire pleasure in them. The verse argues against premature deconstruction.

3.27.title: The illusion of agency
3.27.body: प्रकृतेः क्रियमाणानि गुणैः कर्माणि सर्वशः = "actions are being performed in every way by the guṇas of prakṛti." क्रियमाणानि is passive present participle ("being-done"); तृतीया गुणैः = "by the qualities." The agent is grammatically eliminated — what's left is just the passive event.

3.27.title: कर्ता अहम् इति — the ego-claim
3.27.body: अहंकार-विमूढ-आत्मा कर्ता अहं इति मन्यते = "the one whose self is deluded by ego thinks 'I am the doer'." इति marks the false statement. अहंकार-विमूढ-आत्मा is a बहुव्रीहि chain — "whose self is deluded by I-maker." The ego is named as the grammatical-and-cosmic mistake.

3.28.title: गुणाः गुणेषु — the guṇas in the guṇas
3.28.body: तत्त्व-वित् ... गुणाः गुणेषु वर्तन्ते इति मत्वा न सज्जते = "the knower of truth, thinking 'guṇas turn within guṇas,' is not attached." The doctrine: the senses (one set of guṇas) operate on sense-objects (another set of guṇas) — and the self is not involved. इति marks the quoted realization.

3.28.title: मत्वा — absolutive of the realization
3.28.body: "Having understood." The whole verse hangs on this absolutive: realization → non-attachment. मन् + -त्वा. Krishna's verb-choice puts the cognitive event ahead of the behavioral consequence.

3.29.title: Two-tier pedagogy
3.29.body: Krishna's most counterintuitive instruction: कृत्स्न-विद् न विचालयेत् = "the all-knower should NOT unsettle" those (तान् अकृत्स्न-विदः मन्दान्) who don't see the full picture. विधिलिङ् न विचालयेत् = "should not shake." The wise person practices intellectual restraint, not just sensory restraint.

3.29.title: गुण-सम्मूढाः — deluded by guṇas
3.29.body: बहुव्रीहि: "those whose delusion is by guṇas." तृतीया inside the compound. Krishna names the unwise as belonging-to-prakṛti rather than insulting them — the framing is morphological, not moralistic.

3.30.title: Three bahuvrīhis + one imperative
3.30.body: निराशीः ("with desire-for-results departed"), निर्ममः ("without mine-ness"), विगत-ज्वरः ("whose fever has gone") — three modifiers stacked on "you." Then the imperative युध्यस्व ("fight!") + absolutive भूत्वा ("having become"). Sanskrit packs the preparation + the command into one tight sentence.

3.30.title: मयि सर्वाणि कर्माणि संन्यस्य — the offering
3.30.body: Locative मयि ("in me") + absolutive संन्यस्य ("having renounced into"). The construction is "having dedicated all actions INTO me." The grammar transforms renunciation from "giving up" to "giving to." This single verse seeds the बक्ति framework that BG 9-12 will develop.

3.31.title: Two conditions = two participles
3.31.body: श्रद्धावन्तः and अनसूयन्तः — both present participles (मतुप् and शतृ) modifying "the men who follow my teaching." "Having faith" + "not envying" = the qualifying state. The teaching alone isn't enough — the receiving attitude is grammatically embedded.

3.31.title: मुच्यन्ते कर्मभिः — released from karma
3.31.body: Passive लट् 3pl: "are released BY actions" — but read as "released FROM actions" (तृतीया as instrumental-of-separation). The teaching dissolves the very karma it instructs.

3.32.title: Contrast partner to 3.31
3.32.body: ये एतद् अभ्यसूयन्तः न अनुतिष्ठन्ति मे मतम् = "who, finding fault, do not follow my view." अभ्यसूयन्तः is the OPPOSITE of the अनसूयन्तः in 3.31 — same root + reduplication-marker. Krishna pairs the two verses grammatically to teach the negative example.

3.32.title: नष्टान्, अचेतसः — the verdict
3.32.body: Accusative plurals — both modifiers of तान् ("them"). नष्टान् = "lost / ruined"; अचेतसः = "without consciousness / understanding." Krishna delivers the cosmic verdict by case-agreement, the morphology carrying the moral.

3.33.title: ज्ञानवान् अपि — even the wise
3.33.body: Even the wise person (ज्ञानवान्, मतुप्) acts in accordance with his nature (स्वस्याः प्रकृतेः). The doctrine is humbling: knowledge alone doesn't override प्रकृति. The verse closes: निग्रहः किं करिष्यति = "what will restraint do?" — rhetorical futurity from √कृ.

3.33.title: प्रकृतिं यान्ति भूतानि — the cosmic homing
3.33.body: "Beings go to (their) nature." Locative-direction यान्ति (लट् 3pl of √या). The verb of motion is metaphorical: beings tend toward their own grain. Krishna sets up 3.34 where senses-and-objects are the specific मचानिच्sm to manage.

3.34.title: Locative + dual + locative = the desire structure
3.34.body: इन्द्रियस्य-इन्द्रियस्य अर्थे राग-द्वेषौ व्यवस्थितौ = "in the sense-object of each sense, attraction-aversion are established." Distributive doubling (इन्द्रियस्य-इन्द्रियस्य = "of each sense") + locative अर्थे ("in the object") + द्विवचन राग-द्वेषौ (the pair). The geometry of desire is encoded in case-grammar.

3.34.title: तौ ही अस्य परिपन्थिनौ
3.34.body: "They (तौ — dual) are his (अस्य) waylayers (परिपन्थिनौ — dual)." The dual ending -औ persists throughout. परिपन्थिन् = "who lies on the road" — bandits. Sanskrit makes attraction and aversion into a single two-headed enemy through grammatical duality.

3.35.title: Two comparatives, one ethic
3.35.body: श्रेयान् स्व-धर्मः विगुणः परधर्मात् स्वनुष्ठितात् = "better is one's own dharma, even if flawed, than another's well-performed." Both comparatives govern पञ्चमी ("better than"). Then: स्वधर्मे निधनं श्रेयः = "death in one's own dharma is better." परधर्मो भयावहः = "another's is fear-bringing."

3.35.title: विगुण vs स्वनुष्ठित — quality vs ownership
3.35.body: विगुण ("flawed," lit. "without quality") opposed to स्वनुष्ठित ("well-performed"). Krishna's claim flips intuition: ownership of dharma beats execution-quality of someone else's. The verse is structurally a contrast — and a recurring slogan of the Gītā.

3.36.title: Arjuna's follow-up question
3.36.body: अथ केन प्रयुक्तः अयं पापं चरति पूरुषः अनिच्छन् अपि = "now, by what driven does this person commit sin, even unwilling?" तृतीया केन ("by what") + PPP प्रयुक्तः ("driven, set-going") + present participle अनिच्छन् ("not willing"). The question Krishna will answer for the rest of the chapter.

3.36.title: बलात् इव — "as if by force"
3.36.body: बलात् is ablative of बल ("strength"). इव ("as if") softens: it's not literal force, but feels like it. The verse names experience: sin happens to me, against my wish, as if dragged. Arjuna is asking for the mechanism.

3.37.title: काम → क्रोध — the substitution
3.37.body: Krishna names काम ("desire") and then immediately substitutes क्रोध ("anger") via एष-एष — "this same one." The doctrine: desire and anger are the SAME force, in different states. रजोगुण-समुद्भवः (पञ्चमी तत्पुरुष: "arising from rajas") names the source.

3.37.title: Three modifiers, all bahuvrīhi
3.37.body: महाशनः ("great-eater"), महापाप्मा ("great-sinner"), वैरिणम् ("as the enemy"). The imperative विद्धि एनम् इह वैरिणम् = "know this one as the enemy here" — the द्वितीया एनम् is the object of विद्धि, and वैरिणम् is in apposition (also द्वितीया).

3.38.title: Three similes, one increasing scale
3.38.body: धूमेन वह्निः ("fire by smoke"), आदर्शः मलेन ("mirror by dust"), उल्बेन गर्भः ("embryo by membrane") — three covering relationships, each more total than the last. तेन इदं आवृतम् = "by it (काम), this (knowledge) is covered." Krishna's pedagogical use of triplets escalates the threat.

3.38.title: Passive PPPs as predicates
3.38.body: अव्रियते ("is covered," passive लट् 3sg of आ-वृ) + आवृतः (PPP) + आवृतम् (PPP). Sanskrit uses the passive PPP three times to keep the agent (काम/smoke/dust) in तृतीया and the object (knowledge/fire/embryo) as the subject — it's a covered-thing-foregrounded grammar.

3.39.title: Two extended bahuvrīhi epithets for काम
3.39.body: ज्ञानिनः नित्य-वैरिणा ("perpetual enemy of the wise") + काम-रूपेण ... अनलेन ("of the form of desire ... insatiable fire"). Each in तृतीया — "BY this thing." The verse extends 3.38's enemy-naming with two new epithets to drive the moral home.

3.39.title: दुष्पूरेण — "hard-to-fill"
3.39.body: गति-समास compound: दुस्-पूर्ण-इण = "badly-fillable." Krishna names काम as a fire that can't be sated — drinking gasoline to put it out only makes it bigger. The grammar (compound + abstract noun) packs the impossibility into one word.

3.40.title: Three locations of the enemy
3.40.body: इन्द्रियाणि, मनः, बुद्धिः — three nominatives in apposition, all named as the "seat" (अधिष्ठानम्, predicate nominative) of काम. Krishna locates desire ANATOMICALLY: not abstract but housed in specific layers of the cognitive apparatus.

3.40.title: एतैः विमोहयति — by these it deludes
3.40.body: तृतीया एतैः ("by these — the three seats") + विमोहयति ("deludes," लट् causative 3sg). The instrument is the seats; the action is the deluding; the object is the embodied person (देहिनम्, द्वितीया). Three-way grammar names the assault.

3.41.title: Therefore-imperative + epithet
3.41.body: तस्मात् त्वम् इन्द्रियाणि आदौ नियम्य ... पाप्मानं प्रजहि = "therefore you, having first restrained the senses, kill this sinner." आदौ ("first," locative of आदि as adverbial) + absolutive नियम्य + imperative प्रजहि (लोट् 2sg of प्र-√हन्).

3.41.title: ज्ञान-विज्ञान-नाशनम् — the threat named
3.41.body: द्वितीया apposition to पाप्मानम् / एनम्. ज्ञान-विज्ञान-नाशनम् = "destroyer of knowledge and realization" (षष्ठी तत्पुरुष chain). The reason to kill काम is named morphologically: it destroys both ज्ञान (theoretical) and विज्ञान (practical/realized) knowledge.

3.42.title: The ascending hierarchy
3.42.body: इन्द्रियाणि → मनः → बुद्धिः → सः (= आत्मा). Each lower one is "surpassed by" (परम्) the next. The comparative-with-ablative chain: इन्द्रियेभ्यः परं मनः (पञ्चमी "than the senses") + मनसः परा बुद्धिः + बुद्धेः परतस्तु सः. Sanskrit's case-grammar makes the ladder visible at a glance.

3.42.title: सः — the unspecified self
3.42.body: The chain ends in a bare nominative pronoun सः ("he / that") — Krishna deliberately leaves the highest referent unnamed. The technique is invitational: name the immanent (senses), the intentional (mind), the discriminative (intellect), and then point past them all with a pronoun.

3.43.title: Two absolutives prep the climactic imperative
3.43.body: एवं बुद्धेः परं बुद्ध्वा ("having understood that-which-is-beyond-intellect in this way") + संस्तभ्य आत्मानम् आत्मना ("having stabilized the self by the self") → जहि शत्रुम् ("kill the enemy"). The absolutive chain stages the realization; the imperative lands the act.

3.43.title: काम-रूपं दुरासदम् — closing the chapter on the enemy
3.43.body: द्वितीया apposition to शत्रुम्. कामरूपम् ("of the form of desire"), दुरासदम् ("hard to approach"). The chapter ends with the enemy named, located, and the soldier (Arjuna, addressed महा-बाहो) given the order. Then BG 4 picks up the cosmic frame.
